new_findings.notes: Adopted from DIM-123-001 (wa_session_b_findings id 86); session 20260602 comment-evaluation; rolls into the CHAR-1 T0 layer alongside 11689/11690/11696.
new_findings.finding_text: **[CHAR-1]** E — The pride/majesty dual-register is a structured theological claim about the relationship between divine transcendence and human self-assertion. The core pride vocabulary — ga'ah, ga'on, rum, ramam, and NT kauchaomai ('boasting') — is SHARED between human pride and DIVINE MAJESTY: the same roots name God's legitimate exaltation and the creature's illegitimate self-elevation (cf. the 174 M22-register divine-exaltation verses set aside at Phase 5.5; T0.1.2 / T0.3.2 above). The kauchaomai groups make the structure explicit: boasting is legitimate when directed toward God (the creature glorying in God's majesty) and illegitimate when turned inward toward self. The biblical claim is therefore that pride is precisely the human attempt to claim for oneself what belongs to God's majesty alone — the usurpation of the singular divine prerogative of genuine exaltation (cf. T0.1.1, 11689). The characteristic is not shared with God: the VOCABULARY is shared, but the DISPOSITION is not — divine majesty is proper self-exaltation grounded in being God, whereas human pride is the same posture without the warrant. [Adopted from DIM-123-001.]
